sections.title: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
sections.content:
The CFA Institute Code of Ethics establishes the ethical standards for investment professionals worldwide. It emphasizes integrity, competence, diligence, and professionalism in the investment industry.

The Standards of Professional Conduct provide specific guidance on professional responsibilities and are organized into seven key areas. All CFA members and candidates MUST adhere to these standards.

Why Ethics Matters: The investment industry is built on trust. Clients entrust professionals with their life savings, retirement funds, and financial futures. Ethical conduct ensures this trust is maintained and the capital markets function properly.
keyPoints: Act with integrity, competence, diligence, and in an ethical manner
Place the integrity and interests of clients ABOVE your own interests
Use reasonable care and exercise independent professional judgment
Practice and encourage others to practice in a professional and ethical manner
Promote the integrity and viability of the global capital markets
Maintain and improve professional competence
Standards apply to ALL professional activities, not just investment decisions

practiceQuestions.question: According to the CFA Institute Code of Ethics, when a member's personal interests conflict with client interests, the member should:
options: Balance both interests equally
Place client interests above personal interests
Disclose the conflict and proceed as planned
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Code of Ethics explicitly states that members must place the integrity and interests of clients above their own personal interests. This is a fundamental principle.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six components of the Code of Ethics?
options: Maintain and improve professional competence
Guarantee investment returns to clients
Promote the integrity of capital markets
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Code of Ethics does NOT require guaranteeing returns (which would be impossible and unethical). It focuses on ethical conduct, competence, diligence, and market integrity.

practiceQuestions.question: The CFA Institute Standards of Professional Conduct apply to:
options: Only investment research activities
All professional activities of members and candidates
Only activities involving client accounts
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Standards apply to ALL professional activities, not just investment decisions or client work. This includes teaching, writing, and any activity where the member represents themselves as a finance professional.

sections.title: Professional Misconduct - Standard I(D)
sections.content:
Standard I(D) prohibits professional misconduct in any form. Members must not engage in any conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, or deceit, or commit any act that reflects adversely on their professional reputation, integrity, or competence.

Key Understanding: This standard applies to BOTH professional and personal conduct. A conviction for certain crimes (especially those involving fraud or dishonesty) may constitute a violation even if unrelated to work.

Examples of Violations:

  • Fraudulent business practices
  • Lying to clients or employers
  • Cheating on professional examinations
  • Conduct that calls into question your fitness to serve clients
keyPoints: Dishonesty, fraud, and deceit are STRICTLY PROHIBITED
Conduct reflecting adversely on professional reputation is misconduct
Applies to BOTH professional and personal conduct
Criminal convictions may constitute misconduct (especially fraud-related)
Minor traffic violations typically do NOT violate this standard
Personal bankruptcy alone does NOT violate this standard

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following would MOST LIKELY violate Standard I(D) regarding Professional Misconduct?
options: Being convicted of a misdemeanor traffic violation
Engaging in fraudulent business practices
Filing for personal bankruptcy
practiceQuestions.explanation: Fraudulent business practices involve dishonesty and deceit, which directly violate Standard I(D). Minor traffic violations and personal bankruptcy typically do not reflect on professional integrity.

practiceQuestions.question: Standard I(D) on Professional Misconduct applies to:
options: Only professional activities during work hours
Only activities related to client accounts
Both professional and personal conduct
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard I(D) applies to ANY conduct that reflects adversely on a member's professional reputation, integrity, or competence, whether in professional or personal activities.

sections.title: Knowledge of the Law - Standard I(A)
sections.content:
Standard I(A) requires members to understand and comply with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations (including the CFA Institute Code and Standards) that govern their professional activities.

When Laws Conflict with Standards:

  • If applicable law is MORE STRICT than the Code and Standards → Follow the law
  • If the Code and Standards are MORE STRICT → Follow the Code and Standards
  • If applicable law is LESS STRICT → Still follow the Code and Standards

If You Discover Violations:

  1. Do NOT participate in the violation
  2. Disassociate from the activity
  3. Seek advice from compliance or legal counsel
  4. Consider reporting to appropriate authorities
keyPoints: Must understand and comply with ALL applicable laws and regulations
When conflicts exist, follow the MORE STRICT requirement
Cannot knowingly participate in violations
Should disassociate from violations and document your objection
Inaction + knowledge of violation = potential violation
May need to seek legal counsel and report to authorities

practiceQuestions.question: When there is a conflict between local law and the CFA Code and Standards, a member should:
options: Always follow local law regardless of strictness
Always follow the CFA Code and Standards
Follow the MORE STRICT of the two requirements
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard I(A) requires members to follow the MORE STRICT of applicable law or the Code and Standards when there is a conflict. This ensures the highest ethical standards are maintained.

practiceQuestions.question: If a member discovers their employer is engaging in illegal activities, they should FIRST:
options: Ignore it as it's management's responsibility
Disassociate from the activity and seek guidance from compliance/legal
Immediately report it to the media
practiceQuestions.explanation: Members must NOT knowingly participate in violations. They should disassociate from the activity, seek guidance from compliance or legal counsel, and potentially report to appropriate authorities if the situation is not remedied.

sections.title: Independence and Objectivity - Standard I(B)
sections.content:
Standard I(B) requires members to maintain independence and objectivity in their professional activities. They must not offer, solicit, or accept any gift, benefit, compensation, or consideration that could reasonably be expected to compromise their independence or objectivity.

Gift Guidelines:

  • Modest gifts from clients: Generally acceptable
  • Expensive gifts: Should be declined or require employer permission
  • Gifts intended to influence decisions: Must be declined

Special Situations:

  • Issuer-paid research: Allowed ONLY if compensation is flat fee (not tied to conclusions)
  • Credit rating agencies: Must maintain independence from issuers
  • Investment banking relationships: Must separate research from banking pressure
keyPoints: Maintain independence in investment analysis and recommendations
Avoid conflicts of interest or the APPEARANCE of conflicts
Disclose conflicts to employers and clients
Modest gifts are acceptable; substantial gifts require permission
Issuer-paid research allowed only with flat fee (not performance-based)
Must resist pressure from investment banking or other departments

practiceQuestions.question: A research analyst receives expensive tickets to a sporting event from a company whose stock she covers. According to Standard I(B), she should:
options: Accept the tickets without disclosure
Decline the tickets or seek employer permission before accepting
Accept and sell the tickets for cash
practiceQuestions.explanation: Expensive gifts could compromise independence and objectivity. Members should either decline such gifts or seek employer permission with appropriate disclosure before accepting.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes the requirement for independence and objectivity?
options: Members must never accept any gift from anyone
Members must avoid situations that could reasonably compromise their judgment
Members must only work for one employer
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard I(B) requires members to avoid situations that could REASONABLY be expected to compromise their independence and objectivity. It's about both actual independence and the appearance of independence.

sections.title: Misrepresentation - Standard I(C)
sections.content:
Standard I(C) prohibits members from making any misrepresentations relating to investment analysis, recommendations, actions, or other professional activities.

Types of Misrepresentation:

  • Guaranteeing investment performance (NEVER allowed)
  • Plagiarism (using others' work without attribution)
  • Misstating qualifications or credentials
  • Misrepresenting services or track record
  • Omitting material information that would change the meaning of statements

Plagiarism Prevention:

  • Always attribute quotes and ideas to their source
  • Cite data from recognized sources (government, exchanges, etc.)
  • When in doubt, provide attribution
keyPoints: Cannot make FALSE or MISLEADING statements about investments
CANNOT guarantee specific investment returns (EVER)
Must attribute quotes and ideas to their source (no plagiarism)
Cannot misrepresent qualifications, services, or track record
Factual information from recognized sources need not be attributed
Omissions can be misrepresentation if material information is left out

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following would violate Standard I(C) on Misrepresentation?
options: Presenting a colleague's research as your own without attribution
Disclosing all material risks to clients
Using industry-standard financial models
practiceQuestions.explanation: Presenting someone else's work as your own constitutes plagiarism, which is a form of misrepresentation under Standard I(C). Always attribute work to its original source.

practiceQuestions.question: A portfolio manager tells a client: 'I guarantee you'll earn 15% returns this year.' This statement:
options: Is acceptable if based on strong historical performance
Violates Standard I(C) on Misrepresentation
Is acceptable if the client is sophisticated and understands risks
practiceQuestions.explanation: Guaranteeing specific investment returns is ALWAYS a misrepresentation because investment performance CANNOT be guaranteed. This violates Standard I(C) regardless of client sophistication or past performance.

sections.title: Duties to Clients - Loyalty, Prudence, and Care (III-A)
sections.content:
Standard III(A) requires loyalty to clients and mandates that members place client interests above their own interests and above their employer's interests.

Key Requirements:

  • Act for the benefit of clients
  • Exercise prudence and care in managing client assets
  • Vote proxies in clients' best interests (don't waste proxy votes)
  • Disclose conflicts of interest to clients
  • Seek best execution for client transactions

Client Definition: The client may be the entity that pays you (e.g., pension fund, mutual fund) OR the ultimate beneficiaries (e.g., pension beneficiaries, fund shareholders). Understand who YOUR client is in each situation.
keyPoints: Client interests come BEFORE employer or personal interests
Must exercise prudence and care (the 'prudent person' standard)
Must vote proxies in clients' best interests
Must disclose ALL conflicts of interest to clients
Must seek best execution for client transactions
Understand who your client is (may be the entity OR beneficiaries)

practiceQuestions.question: When a conflict arises between a member's personal interests and client interests, the member should:
options: Prioritize personal interests
Prioritize client interests
Split the difference equally
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(A) on Loyalty, Prudence, and Care requires members to place client interests above their own personal interests. This is a fundamental duty.

practiceQuestions.question: Regarding proxy voting, members should:
options: Always vote with management recommendations
Vote proxies in the best interests of clients
Not vote proxies as it creates conflicts
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(A) requires members to vote proxies in the best interests of clients. This means analyzing each proposal and voting accordingly, not automatically following management.

sections.title: Fair Dealing - Standard III(B)
sections.content:
Standard III(B) requires members to deal fairly and objectively with all clients when providing investment analysis, making investment recommendations, taking investment action, or engaging in other professional activities.

What Fair Dealing Means:

  • NOT treating all clients exactly the same
  • But NOT discriminating against any clients
  • Different service levels are acceptable (if disclosed)
  • All clients should have FAIR OPPORTUNITY to act on recommendations

Best Practices:

  • Disseminate recommendations simultaneously to all clients who might be interested
  • Don't tell favored clients first
  • Use broad distribution methods (email, website, etc.)
keyPoints: Must treat all clients FAIRLY (not necessarily EQUALLY)
Cannot favor some clients over others for recommendations
Different service levels acceptable if disclosed in advance
Disseminate recommendations to all interested clients simultaneously
Don't selectively disclose to favored clients first
Apply to both recommendations AND investment actions

practiceQuestions.question: A firm is issuing a buy recommendation for a stock. To comply with Standard III(B) on Fair Dealing, the firm should:
options: Tell favored clients first, then others
Make the recommendation available to all clients who might be interested at approximately the same time
Only tell clients who have large accounts
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(B) requires members to deal fairly and objectively with all clients when disseminating investment recommendations. All clients who might be interested should have fair opportunity to receive and act on the recommendation.

practiceQuestions.question: Standard III(B) on Fair Dealing allows for:
options: Different service levels for different clients if disclosed
Telling some clients about recommendations before others
Charging all clients the same fees regardless of service
practiceQuestions.explanation: Fair dealing does NOT mean identical treatment. Different service levels (and fees) are acceptable as long as they are disclosed to all clients and no client is discriminated against.

sections.title: Suitability - Standard III(C)
sections.content:
Standard III(C) requires members to make a reasonable inquiry into a client's or prospective client's investment experience, risk and return objectives, and financial constraints BEFORE making investment recommendations or taking investment action.

Required Information (IPS Elements):

  • Return objectives
  • Risk tolerance
  • Time horizon
  • Liquidity needs
  • Tax considerations
  • Legal and regulatory constraints
  • Unique circumstances

When Managing a Portfolio:

  • Must judge suitability in context of the ENTIRE portfolio
  • Individual investments may be risky but suitable as part of diversified portfolio
  • Must update client information regularly
keyPoints: Must understand client's investment objectives BEFORE recommending
Must assess client's risk tolerance
Must consider time horizon, liquidity needs, tax situation
Recommendations must be suitable for client's ENTIRE portfolio
Must update client information REGULARLY
Apply to BOTH recommendations AND investment actions

practiceQuestions.question: Before making an investment recommendation, a member must:
options: Only consider the investment's expected return
Make a reasonable inquiry into the client's investment objectives and constraints
Recommend the same portfolio to all clients
practiceQuestions.explanation: Standard III(C) requires members to make a reasonable inquiry into a client's investment experience, objectives, and constraints before making recommendations. This ensures recommendations are suitable.

practiceQuestions.question: Which client information is LEAST important for determining investment suitability?
options: Client's risk tolerance
Client's investment time horizon
Client's favorite industry sector
practiceQuestions.explanation: While client preferences may be considered, risk tolerance and time horizon are ESSENTIAL for suitability determinations. Favorite sectors are less critical and should not override suitability requirements.

sections.title: Performance Presentation - Standard III(D)
sections.content:
Standard III(D) requires members to make reasonable efforts to ensure that investment performance information is fair, accurate, and complete. Members must not misrepresent past performance or reasonably expected performance.

Prohibited Practices:

  • Cherry-picking only best-performing accounts
  • Presenting gross returns without disclosing fees
  • Imputing past performance to new funds without disclosure
  • Using hypothetical performance without clear labeling
  • Omitting relevant information that would affect interpretation

Best Practices:

  • Present composite performance (not just selected accounts)
  • Disclose whether performance is gross or net of fees
  • Disclose if performance is hypothetical or simulated
  • Follow GIPS standards when possible
keyPoints: Cannot misrepresent past or expected performance
Must present performance FAIRLY, ACCURATELY, and COMPLETELY
Cannot cherry-pick only best-performing accounts
Must disclose if performance is gross or net of fees
Must clearly label hypothetical or simulated performance
Should follow GIPS (Global Investment Performance Standards)

practiceQuestions.question: A portfolio manager advertises only their best-performing account from the past year. This practice:
options: Is acceptable marketing
Violates Standard III(D) on Performance Presentation
Is required by regulations
practiceQuestions.explanation: Cherry-picking only the best performance without context misrepresents overall performance and violates Standard III(D). Performance
